answer:
**In the *Manusmṛti*, the Brahmin's unique authority to teach sacred knowledge (the Veda) is explicitly tied both to birth into the highest *varṇa* and to the exclusive duty of the "twice-born" (*dvija*) classes, from which only Brahmins may instruct others:**

> The three *varnas* of the twice-born, abiding in their own duties, may study; but a Brahmana alone should teach them, not the other two; this is the decision.[^1]

**The *Yājñavalkya Smṛti* similarly structures authority hierarchically by birth into *varṇas*, with Brahmins at the apex for Vedic rites (*śrauta smārta rites*) and daily studies, as signalled in its distinctive phrasing (though no single verse isolates "birth + Veda command" as starkly as Manu 10.1; the work repeatedly enumerates *varṇa*-specific duties starting with Brahmins).**[^2]

**Cynewulf's *Elene* depicts clerical authority (bishop/priest) as derived from faith, baptism, and installation within the Christian community—the "body" of Christ—rather than birth. Judas (renamed Cyriacus) transitions from Jew to bishop via revelation, baptism, and appointment by Eusebius:**

> Indeed, fate decreed that he so faithful and so dear to God in this worldly kingdom should become, pleasing to Christ. That was made known, after Elene commanded Eusebius, the bishop of Rome, a man of deep counsel, to be fetched for support... that he might install in the priesthood in Jerusalem Judas as bishop for the people.[^3]

The Brahmin office is harder for an outsider to assume. Birth into the Brahmin *varṇa* is immutable (a Shudra or non-twice-born cannot become one, per the *varṇāśrama* system), whereas Veda mastery is restricted but secondary to heredity.[^4] A Christian priestly role requires conversion ("pleasing to Christ" via faith/baptism) and ordination, both accessible to outsiders (as with Judas), embodying membership in Christ's body (the Church). Hereditary caste trumps acquirable spiritual status.

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, ch. 10, [p-4999b4](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/10/translation/#p-4999b4).

[^2]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, ch. 1, [full wiki](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/wiki/full/), highlighting "vedic study," "śrauta smārta rites," and *varṇa* ordering (e.g., "brāhmaṇa kṣatriya vaiśya").

[^3]: Cynewulf, *Elene*, ch. 1, [p-f5d1f1](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-f5d1f1) (context: Judas's faith makes him "inside Christ's body" via baptism/spiritual rebirth; bishopric follows).

[^4]: *Manusmṛti*, ch. 10 opening, [p-4999b4](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/10/translation/#p-4999b4); birth primacy recurs (e.g., Mn_2.19 on Brahmana-born conduct).
